questions.question:
What happens when you run this code?

def square(n):
    return n * n

assert square(3) == 9
assert square(-2) == 4
print("done")

options.explanation: Correct. Both assertions are true — square(3) is 9 and square(-2) is 4 — so neither raises AssertionError. The script reaches print("done") and runs it.

options.text: It raises AssertionError on the second assert.
options.explanation: square(-2) is (-2) * (-2) = 4, which equals 4, so the second assert passes. AssertionError only fires when the condition is false.

options.text: It raises AssertionError on the first assert.
options.explanation: square(3) is 9, which equals 9, so the first assert passes. Both assertions in this code are correct.

options.text: Nothing happens — assert statements are always ignored.
options.explanation: assert statements run normally unless you start Python with the -O flag. In regular mode, a failing assert raises AssertionError and stops the program.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly tests that a function raises ValueError when passed a negative number?

options.explanation: assertEqual checks that two values are equal. It does not catch exceptions — if my_func(-1) raises ValueError, this call itself crashes with that error before assertEqual can compare anything.
This calls my_func(-1), which would raise ValueError immediately. The assert never runs. Even if it did, comparing a raised exception to the class itself would not work as expected.
Correct. assertRaises used as a context manager catches the expected exception. If my_func(-1) raises ValueError, the test passes. If it raises something else or raises nothing, the test fails.
This swallows the exception but never asserts anything. If my_func(-1) raises no exception at all, the try block exits silently and the test still passes — which is the opposite of what you want.

options.text: It makes greet() only callable from within the same file.
options.explanation: The guard has nothing to do with function visibility. greet() can still be imported and called from any other file regardless of the guard.

options.text: It prevents the print() from running when this file is imported by another module.
options.explanation: Correct. When Python imports a file, __name__ is set to the module's name, not "__main__", so the block is skipped. When you run the file directly, __name__ is "__main__" and the block runs. This lets the file serve as both a runnable script and an importable module.

options.text: It makes the program run faster by skipping the function definition.
options.explanation: Python always reads the entire file, including function definitions. The guard only controls whether the block of code inside it executes — it has no effect on what gets defined.

options.text: It checks whether the greet function returned the right value.
options.explanation: The guard checks the value of __name__, a special variable Python sets when loading a file. It has nothing to do with return values.
